preamble: The devoicing of the voiced postalveolar fricative [ʒ] (zh) into the voiceless [ʃ] (sh) is a frequent phonetic process. It often occurs as a result of final-obstruent devoicing or regressive assimilation to adjacent voiceless consonants, driven by the aerodynamic difficulty of maintaining voicing during frication.

examples.note: Final-obstruent devoicing: /ʒ/ is realized as voiceless word-finally.
Assimilation: /ʒ/ can become [ʃ] under the influence of the preceding voiceless [k].

sources: Ohala, J. J. (1983). The origin of sound patterns in vocal tract constraints.
Jones, D. (1917). An English Pronouncing Dictionary.
